hints.Arma: 'Arma' is a neuter plural noun meaning 'arms/weapons'

hints.to fall: 'to fall' is 'cadere' — similar start but different verb
hints.to seize: 'to seize' is 'capere' — 'cano' means to sing or chant

hints.2nd singular: 2nd singular would be 'canis' — the -o ending is 1st singular
hints.3rd singular: 3rd singular would be 'canit' — the -o ending is 1st person
hints.1st plural: 1st plural would be 'canimus' — 'cano' has no plural marker

hints.capio, capere, cepi, captum: These are for 'capere' (to seize) — note the different perfect stem
hints.curro, currere, cucurri, cursum: These are for 'currere' (to run) — a different 3rd conj. verb
hints.cado, cadere, cecidi, casum: These are for 'cadere' (to fall) — similar reduplication but different verb

hints.but: 'but' is 'sed' or 'autem' — '-que' is a joining particle
hints.or: 'or' is 'vel' or 'aut' — '-que' links, it doesn't offer alternatives

hints.Nominative: Neut. pl. nom. and acc. look the same, but 'Arma' is the object of 'cano'
hints.Vocative: Vocative is for direct address — you wouldn't address weapons

hints.Nominative: Nominative of 'vir' is 'vir' — 'virum' with -um ending is accusative
hints.Genitive: Genitive of 'vir' is 'viri' — the -um ending is accusative singular
hints.Ablative: Ablative of 'vir' is 'viro' — the -um ending is accusative

hints.Nominative: Nominative of 'Troia' is 'Troia' — the -ae ending is gen. or dat.
hints.Dative: Dative is possible with -ae, but here it means 'of Troy' — possession/origin
hints.Ablative: Ablative of 'Troia' would be 'Troia' — the -ae ending is genitive

hints.Interrogative: 'qui' as interrogative would start a question — this is a statement
hints.Demonstrative: Demonstratives are 'hic', 'ille', 'is' — 'qui' introduces a relative clause
hints.Conjunction: Conjunctions join clauses without modifying a noun — 'qui' refers back to 'virum'

hints.Arma: 'Arma' is neuter plural — 'primus' is masc. singular, so it can't modify it
hints.Troiae: 'Troiae' is a proper noun — 'primus' modifies the man being described
hints.oris: 'oris' is neut. pl. — 'primus' is masc. sing., doesn't agree

hints.Nominative: Nom. plural of 'ora' is 'orae' (1st decl.) — 'oris' is ablative
hints.Genitive: This form could be gen. sing. of 'os, oris' (mouth), but here it's abl. pl. of 'ora' (shore)
hints.Dative: Dative plural of 'ora' would also be 'oris', but 'ab' governs ablative

hints.Accusative: 'ab' always takes ablative — prepositions like 'ad' take accusative
hints.Genitive: No Latin preposition governs the genitive directly
hints.Dative: 'ab' takes ablative, not dative — they're different cases

translation: Arms and the man I sing, of Troy, who first from the shores
